macro_segments.title: The Validation of Documents (Kiyum Shtarot) by a Beit Din of Three: The Challenge Process When One Judge's Validity is Questioned

micro_segments.title: Overview: The Principle of Peh She'Asar and the Transition to Documentary Validation
Three Judges Convening to Validate: The Rule That Before Signing, Testimony About a Third Judge's Validity Can Be Heard
The Two Types of Challenge to a Judge's Validity: Theft (Gazlanut) vs. Personal Status (Pgam Mishpacha)
The Doctrine of Giluy Milta (Disclosure of a Matter): Why Testimony About Personal Status is Not Considered True Testimony in the Same Way
Reconciling the Contradiction: Two Witnesses Say One Judge is a Thief; The Other Two Claim He Repented (Aved Teshuvah)
The Distinction Between Before and After Signing: Why Testimony About a Third Judge's Validity Cannot Be Heard After the Document Is Signed

macro_segments.title: Practical Case: A Beit Din of Three Where One Judge Dies Before Signing—The Required Notation and the Language of Official Authority

micro_segments.title: The Requirement to Declare 'We Were Three': Why Merely Writing 'Beit Din' Is Insufficient Without Explicit Reference to Three Judges
Alternative Formulation: Invoking Official Authority—Writing 'The Beit Din of Rav Ashi' or 'Rav Ashi Commanded Us'
Concern About a Brazen (Chatzuf) Beit Din of Two: Why Explicit Language of Three or Authority Is Necessary to Dispel Suspicion
Conclusion: The Technical Language of Documentary Validation Reflects Historical Practice in Geonic Courts

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Peh She'Asar (The Mouth That Forbids Is the Mouth That Permits): Its Biblical Derivation and Logical Basis

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Moving from Documentary Validation to the Broader Question of When Personal Statements About Status Are Believed
Case 1: A Woman Says She Was Married, Then Claims She Is Now Divorced—The Application of Peh She'Asar
Case 2: A Woman Taken Captive Claims She Was Not Raped—Peh She'Asar Applied to Presumptions About Captivity
The Source of Peh She'Asar: Is It from Scripture or Merely from Logical Reasoning (Sevara)?
The Gemara's Conclusion: The Principle Is Primarily Based on Logical Reasoning (Sevara), Not Scriptural Necessity
Biblical Derivation: The Father's Declaration About His Daughter's Marriage in the Laws of Motzi Shem Ra

macro_segments.title: Retracting an Earlier Statement About Personal Status: The Doctrine That a Person Can Retract If They Provide a Reasonable Explanation (Amtla)

micro_segments.title: A Woman First Claims to Be Married, Then Retracts and Says She Is Single—When Is She Believed?
The Problem: How Can She Retract When Her Initial Claim Made Her Forbidden (Shavya L'Nafshah Chatichah D'Isura)?
The Solution: If She Provides a Reasonable Explanation for Her Original Claim, She Is Believed to Retract
Historical Example: A Beautiful Woman Who Falsely Claimed Marriage to Deter Unsuitable Suitors, Then Married When Proper Suitors Appeared
Application to Niddah Status: A Woman Claims to Be Niddah, Then Retracts—Special Considerations Regarding Ongoing Marital Dynamics

macro_segments.title: Contradictory Witnesses Regarding Death or Divorce: Two Say the Husband Died vs. Two Say He Did Not; Two Say Divorced vs. Two Say Not Divorced

micro_segments.title: The Rule for Death: Two Witnesses Say Husband Died, Two Say He Did Not—Woman Cannot Marry Beforehand; If She Does Marry, She Does Not Have to Divorce (Tanna Kamma)
The Rule for Divorce: Two Say Divorced, Two Say Not Divorced—Woman Cannot Marry Beforehand; If She Does Marry, She Must Divorce (Rav Menachem bar Rav Yossi)
Explanation via Rav Sheshet: She Marries One of the Witnesses Who Testified the Husband Died, and She Declares 'Bari Li' (I Am Certain)
The Role of Personal Knowledge vs. Beit Din Authority: Why Beit Din Does Not Intervene When Individuals Take Personal Responsibility for Their Status
Explanation via Abaye: One Witness vs. One Witness (Not Two vs. Two)—Special Rules Apply Because One Testimony of Death Equals Two Testimonies
Explanation via Rava: Two Against Two in Both Cases, But Different Consequences Based on the Nature of the Claim
The Distinction Between Misah (Death) and Gerushin (Divorce): Misah Cannot Be Denied if Husband Appears; Gerushin Can Always Be Contradicted by the Husband's Denial
Conclusion: Why the Woman Is More Trustworthy in the Death Case and Thus May Remain Married Even After the Fact

topical_tags.term: Peh She'Asar (The Mouth That Forbids Is the Mouth That Permits)
Kiyum Shtarot (Document Validation by Beit Din)
Giluy Milta (Disclosure of a Matter—Information That Will Become Public)
Gazlanut (Theft as a Challenge to a Witness's Validity)
Aved Teshuvah (Repentance by One Who Stole)
Nogeia B'Eidus (Being a Party to One's Own Testimony; Bias)
Chatzuf Beit Din (Insolent or Brazen Beit Din of Two)
Shavya L'Nafshah Chatichah D'Isura (Making Oneself Forbidden)
Amtla (Reasonable Explanation for Retracting a Statement)
Bari Li (I Am Certain; Personal Knowledge)
Asham Talui (Suspensive Guilt Offering for Doubtful Transgressions)
Tres V'Tres Ninhu (Two Against Two Witnesses)
Yevamot (Halakhot Regarding a Widow Waiting for Levirate Marriage)
Motzi Shem Ra (Spreading False Report About a Woman's Virginity)
